nativeRange.habitat: Occurs in schools above reef drop-offs around oceanic islands and along continental coasts, typically at depths from roughly 6 to 130 m. The species has an unusual antitropical Pacific distribution — found in both northern and southern temperate zones rather than continuously throughout the tropics.

diet.notes: Feeds primarily on zooplankton in the wild and has been documented schooling above reef drop-offs to pick plankton. In captivity it is generally advised to offer varied meaty foods — chopped shrimp, squid, krill, mysis, clams, and small fish — two or three times daily. Krill is commonly recommended as a regular item because of its carotenoid content, which tends to help maintain the species' bright yellow and red coloration. Periodically offering hard-shelled foods is generally advised to help wear down continuously growing teeth.

goodWith: other semi-aggressive fish of similar or larger size
avoidWith: other triggerfish (especially conspecifics outside a bonded pair)

summary: The Crosshatch Triggerfish (Xanthichthys mento) is a planktivorous Pacific species prized for its yellow body, dark crosshatch scale pattern, blue facial lines, and — in males — a vivid red caudal fin. It is among the more reef-tolerant triggerfish, though small ornamental shrimp remain at risk.
careNotes:
The Crosshatch Triggerfish (Xanthichthys mento) is widely considered one of the more reef-compatible members of the family Balistidae — it has been documented largely ignoring sessile invertebrates and even most corals, though larger individuals have been reported eating ornamental shrimp and small crustaceans. Reef-keepers generally treat it as reef-safe with caution rather than fully reef-safe. The species is strikingly sexually dimorphic: males develop a vivid red caudal fin and more intense yellow flanks, while juveniles and females have yellow tails. Males also tend to be slightly larger.

In the wild the species occurs in an unusual antitropical Pacific distribution — found in southern Japan and Hawaii in the north, and in southern California, the Revillagigedo Islands, Clipperton Island, Easter Island, and the Pitcairn Islands in the south — schooling above reef drop-offs and feeding on zooplankton. This planktivorous ecology is partly why it tends to be more peaceful in captivity than most triggerfish, though it has been known to rearrange rockwork and substrate, bite tank equipment (including silicone and rubber fittings), and produce audible grunting sounds when alarmed.

Temperament reports vary among keepers. A single male can be kept with one or more females; housing multiple males in the same aquarium is generally not advised. Pairs have been bred in captivity, though only rarely.

A tank footprint of at least 180 cm is generally advised, as this species is an active swimmer and benefits more from horizontal length than from height. Like all members of the family Balistidae, the Crosshatch Triggerfish has strong lockable dorsal spines used for wedging itself into crevices when threatened, though these spines are not venomous.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has been reported only rarely in private hobbyist tanks. In the wild, males establish spawning and parental-care territories, with females actively tending the eggs. Fecundity has been estimated at roughly 32,000 eggs per spawning cluster. Pairs can be set up by housing one male with one or more females in a large aquarium with abundant rockwork.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Collection page confirmed listing; the species product page (liveaquaria.com/product/1418) returned full husbandry details — care level (easy), temperament (aggressive), max size 11 in, min tank 180 gal, temp 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, diet (carnivore meaty), reef-safe with caution.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Balistidae / Tetraodontiformes), max size 30 cm, depth 6–130 m, antitropical Pacific distribution, planktivorous diet (trophic level 4.0), wild temperature 21.1–29°C, and breeding behaviour (male territorial spawning, ~32,000 eggs/cluster). FishBase uses 'Redtail Triggerfish' as the English vernacular for X. mento — distinct from X. ringens 'Red-Tail Trigger'; the aquarium-trade common name 'Crosshatch Triggerfish' is used here.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked max size 29 cm, salinity ~1.026, water temperature ~26°C, distribution. Reported minimum tank 1,500 L (treated as a long-term / pair recommendation and averaged with LiveAquaria's 180 gal; final value biased toward the larger side per playbook). Confirmed reef-safe with caution and that pairs have bred in captivity.

additional.name: Fishkeeper UK
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size 29 cm, temperature 23–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, reef-safe with caution, diet (frozen meaty + spirulina), sexual dimorphism (males red caudal, females yellow), and moderately-aggressive temperament. Helped average temperament toward semi-aggressive.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has been reported only rarely. The species is oviparous, with males establishing territorial spawning sites and females guarding the eggs. Pairs can be set up by housing one male with one or more females in a suitably large aquarium.
